message.content:
Feature: MHPCO Claim Office - Policy management system with quote and claim operations
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Requirements: From prompt.md - quote computes premiums for insurance items, claim processes damage reports. Includes item values, premium modifiers, claim processing with deductibles and caps, CLI interface reading JSON from stdin and writing JSON to stdout.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, no HITL checkpoints.

content.text: I'll start by reading the specification file to understand the full requirements.

content.content:
1	# MHPCO Claim Office Kata
2	
3	## Setting
4	
5	You are building the policy management system for the **Most Honorable
6	Privileged Claims Office for Magical Risks and Cursed Items** (MHPCO),
7	an old-fashioned insurance institution that covers magical items —
8	swords, amulets, staves, potions, and components such as runes and
9	moonstones. The MHPCO is bureaucratic, slightly stingy, and proud of
10	its venerable traditions.
11	
12	## Feature
13	
14	The system supports two operations: **quote** computes the premium for
15	a list of items a customer wishes to insure, and **claim** processes a
16	damage report against an existing policy.
17	
18	## Rules
19	
20	### Item values and base premiums
21	
22	Each main item has an insurance value and a base premium according to
23	the MHPCO price list:
24	
25	- Sword: 1000 G insurance value, 100 G base premium
26	- Amulet: 600 G / 60 G
27	- Staff: 800 G / 80 G
28	- Potion: 400 G / 40 G
29	
30	Components — for example runes and moonstones — are insured at 250 G
31	each, with a base premium of 25 G per component. A building block of
32	3 alike components is offered at a special base premium of 60 G.
33	
34	### Premium modifiers
35	
36	- Cursed items add a 50 % risk surcharge.
37	- Highly enchanted items (enchantment level ≥ 5) add a 30 % risk
38	  surcharge.
39	- Long-standing customers (≥ 2 years of business with MHPCO) receive a
40	  20 % loyalty discount.
41	- A first insurance carries a 10 % initial assessment surcharge.
42	- Customers receive a 15 % discount on each contract after their first.
43	- A 5 G processing fee is added to every premium.
44	
45	All amounts are rounded to whole G in the MHPCO's favor.
46	
47	### Claim processing
48	
49	A deductible of 100 G applies per damage event. The total payout per
50	policy is capped at twice the insurance sum.
51	
52	- Damage to items with enchantment level ≥ 8 is reimbursed at 50 % of
53	  the damage amount.
54	- Damage to items made of dragon material is fully reimbursed.
55	
56	## Examples
57	
58	The examples below illustrate single rules in isolation. Values shown
59	are base premiums or raw payout amounts before integration with other
60	modifiers, unless stated otherwise.
61	
62	### Building block of 3 alike components
63	
64	- 2 runes → 50 G base premium
65	- 3 runes → 60 G base premium (block applies)
66	- 4 runes → 100 G base premium (no block — block requires exactly 3)
67	- 7 runes → 175 G base premium
68	
69	### "Alike" components
70	
71	> ❓ Hmm — "alike" components: does that mean exactly the same type,
72	> or just the same family (rune-y, gemstone-y)?
73	
74	- 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 G base premium (no block: different types)
75	- 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G base premium (two separate blocks)
76	
77	### Modifier scope on multi-item policies
78	
79	> ❓ A policy with several items: does the cursed surcharge apply to
80	> the whole policy, or just to the cursed item?
81	
82	- a policy covers a cursed sword (base premium 100 G) and a plain
83	  amulet (base premium 60 G) → policy base premium 160 G; the cursed
84	  surcharge adds 50 G (50 % of the cursed sword's base premium, not
85	  of the policy total) → 210 G before further modifiers and fee
86	- item-specific modifiers (cursed, high enchantment) apply to the
87	  base premium of the affected item; policy-wide modifiers (loyalty,
88	  first insurance, follow-up contract) apply to the policy base
89	  premium (the sum of all item base premiums); the processing fee is
90	  added at the very end
91	
92	### Modifier thresholds
93	
94	- customer with exactly 2 years with MHPCO → loyalty discount applies
95	- sword with exactly enchantment 5 → high-enchantment surcharge
96	  applies; if cursed, both surcharges apply
97	- sword with enchantment 4 → no high-enchantment surcharge; curse
98	  surcharge applies only if cursed
99	- dragon-material sword with exactly enchantment 8, damage 1000 G →
100	  payout 400 G (high-enchantment clause applies, then deductible)
101	
102	### Deductible per damage event
103	
104	- a dragon attack damages an insured sword (500 G) and an insured
105	  amulet (300 G); payout = 600 G (the 100 G deductible applies once
106	  per damaged item)
107	
108	### Standard reimbursement (no special clauses)
109	
110	- regular sword (steel, enchantment 3), damage 500 G → payout 400 G
111	  (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; no special clause applies)
112	- damage to a rune (insurance value 250 G), damage 200 G → payout
113	  100 G (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; runes have no
114	  enchantment level or material, so no special clause applies)
115	
116	### Enchantment threshold vs. dragon material
117	
118	- dragon-material sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
119	  (both clauses apply; the 50 % rule wins, then deductible: 500 − 100)
120	- dragon-material sword, enchantment 5, damage 800 G → payout 700 G
121	  (only the dragon-material clause applies: full reimbursement,
122	  then deductible: 800 − 100)
123	- steel sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
124	  (only the high-enchantment clause applies: 50 % first, then
125	  deductible: 500 − 100)
126	
127	### Multiple items of the same type
128	
129	> ❓ What if a customer brings two of the same item — one in hand,
130	> one in the backpack? Or just two swords because they fight with
131	> two?
132	
133	- a policy covers two swords → insurance sum 2000 G (= 2×1000),
134	  cap 4000 G
135	- a dragon attack damages both swords; `damages` contains two
136	  `{itemType: "sword", ...}` entries → each entry is treated as a
137	  separate damage with its own deductible
138	- if the `damages` array contains more entries of a given type than
139	  the policy actually covers (e.g. two sword damages but only one
140	  sword insured) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code; the
141	  whole claim is rejected
142	
143	### Cap exhaustion
144	
145	- a policy covers a sword and an amulet → insurance sum 1600 G
146	  (= 1000 + 600, the sum of the items' insurance values), cap 3200 G
147	- a cursed sword (insurance value 1000 G, premium with modifiers
148	  165 G) → cap 2000 G (based on the unmodified insurance value;
149	  premium modifiers do not raise the cap)
150	- a policy covers a sword and 3 runes (a block) → insurance sum
151	  1750 G (= 1000 + 3×250); the block discount affects the premium
152	  only, not the insurance sum
153	- a sword is insured (insurance sum 1000 G, cap 2000 G); two successive
154	  claims of 1500 G each
155	- first claim → payout 1400 G, cap remaining 600 G
156	- second claim → payout 600 G, cap remaining 0 G (the desired 1400 G
157	  is reduced to the remaining cap)
158	
159	### Rounding in the MHPCO's favor
160	
161	- a premium calculation that yields 197.5 G → final premium 198 G
162	  (rounded up)
163	- a payout calculation that yields 350.5 G → final payout 350 G
164	  (rounded down)
165	- intermediate amounts during a calculation are kept as fractions;
166	  only the final premium or payout is rounded
167	
168	### Edge cases
169	
170	- empty item list → premium 5 G (only the processing fee)
171	- quote includes an item with an unknown type (e.g.
172	  `{type: "broomstick"}`) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
173	  and writes an error description to stderr; no `results` are written
174	  to stdout
175	- claim references a damage entry whose item is not part of the policy
176	  (e.g. an amulet damaged when only a sword is insured, or an item
177	  with an unknown type) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
178	  and writes an error description to stderr
179	- claim contains a damage entry with `amount: -200` → the CLI exits
180	  with a non-zero status code and writes an error description to
181	  stderr
182	
183	## Integration examples
184	
185	The following two examples combine multiple rules to clarify how
186	modifiers stack and how customer history affects the premium.
187	
188	### Newcomer with a cursed sword
189	
190	- customer: 0 years with MHPCO, no previous contract
191	- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 3)
192	- premium: 165 G
193	- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 10 G first insurance = 160 G + 5 G fee = 165 G)
194	
195	### Long-standing customer's second contract
196	
197	> ❓ Wait — does "first insurance" mean the customer's first ever
198	> contract, or the first time we see this particular item? A
199	> long-standing customer with a brand-new sword: does the first
200	> insurance surcharge still apply?
201	
202	- customer: 3 years with MHPCO; this is the customer's second `quote`
203	  in the scenario
204	- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 7)
205	- premium: 160 G
206	- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 30 G high enchantment − 20 G loyalty
207	  + 10 G first insurance − 15 G follow-up contract = 155 G + 5 G fee
208	  = 160 G)
209	- The first insurance surcharge still applies to the new sword, even
210	  though the customer is on a follow-up contract — each item in a
211	  `quote` is treated as a first insurance, regardless of customer
212	  history.
213	
214	## CLI input/output format
215	
216	The implementation must be exposed as a command-line executable
217	`claim-office`. It reads a JSON document from stdin describing a
218	scenario and writes a JSON document with the corresponding results to
219	stdout.
220	
221	A scenario is a JSON object with a `customer` object and a `steps`
222	array. The `customer` describes the single customer for the whole
223	scenario. Each step has an `op` field (`"quote"` or `"claim"`) and the
224	operation-specific input fields. Steps are processed sequentially;
225	later `claim` steps refer to a policy created by an earlier `quote`
226	step via its zero-based step index in a `policy` field.
227	
228	Stdout receives a JSON object with a `results` array of the same length
229	and order as the input `steps`. Each result contains:
230	
231	- For a `quote` step: `premium` (integer, total premium in G).
232	- For a `claim` step: `payout` (integer, payout in G) and
233	  `remainingCap` (integer, cap remaining on the policy after this
234	  claim).
235	
236	### Schema example
237	
238	Stdin:
239	
240	```json
241	{
242	  "customer": {"yearsWithMHPCO": 5},
243	  "steps": [
244	    {
245	      "op": "quote",
246	      "items": [
247	        {"type": "amulet", "material": "silver", "enchantment": 2, "cursed": false}
248	      ]
249	    },
250	    {
251	      "op": "claim",
252	      "policy": 0,
253	      "incident": {
254	        "cause": "fire",
255	        "damages": [
256	          {"itemType": "amulet", "amount": 200}
257	        ]
258	      }
259	    }
260	  ]
261	}
262	```
263	
264	Stdout shape:
265	
266	```json
267	{"results": [{"premium": <integer>}, {"payout": <integer>, "remainingCap": <integer>}]}
268	```
269	
270	## Expected Output Files
271	
272	- Source files implementing the logic and the `claim-office` CLI
273	- Test files
274	- CLI entry point at `src/cli.ts` (reads JSON from stdin, writes JSON to stdout)
275	
276	## Constraints
277	
278	- Use TypeScript for the implementation
279	- The CLI must read JSON from stdin and write JSON to stdout
280	- The CLI entry point must be at `src/cli.ts`
281	- Internal data structures and module organization are free
282

content.text: Now let me check the project setup files.
Good. Now I have a thorough understanding of the specification. Let me create the example mapping file and the test list.

input.content:
# MHPCO Claim Office -- Example Mapping

## Operations

Two operations: **quote** (compute premium) and **claim** (process damage report).

---

## Rules

### R1: Item values and base premiums
- Sword: 1000 G insurance value, 100 G base premium
- Amulet: 600 G / 60 G
- Staff: 800 G / 80 G
- Potion: 400 G / 40 G
- Components (runes, moonstones, etc.): 250 G insurance value, 25 G base premium each

### R2: Component building block
- A group of exactly 3 alike components gets a special base premium of 60 G (instead of 3 x 25 = 75 G).
- "Alike" means exactly the same component type (rune vs moonstone are different types).
- Only groups of exactly 3 qualify; 4 of the same type = 4 x 25 = 100 G (no block).
- Multiple blocks of different types are allowed (e.g., 3 runes + 3 moonstones = 60 + 60 = 120 G).

### R3: Premium modifiers -- item-specific
- **Cursed**: +50% surcharge on the cursed item's base premium.
- **High enchantment** (enchantment >= 5): +30% surcharge on that item's base premium.
- Both can apply to the same item (they stack additively on the base premium).

### R4: Premium modifiers -- policy-wide
- **Loyalty discount** (customer yearsWithMHPCO >= 2): -20% of the policy base premium (sum of all item base premiums).
- **First insurance surcharge**: +10% of the policy base premium. Every item in a quote is treated as a first insurance regardless of customer history.
- **Follow-up contract discount**: -15% of the policy base premium on each contract after the customer's first quote in the scenario.

### R5: Processing fee
- 5 G processing fee added at the very end of every premium calculation.

### R6: Modifier application order
- Item-specific modifiers (cursed, high enchantment) apply to each item's base premium.
- Policy-wide modifiers (loyalty, first insurance, follow-up) apply to the policy base premium (sum of all item base premiums before item-specific modifiers? No -- the policy base premium is the sum of item base premiums).
- Wait, looking at integration example 1: 100 G base + 50 G curse + 10 G first insurance = 160 G.
  - The 10 G first insurance is 10% of 100 G (the item base premium, which is also the policy base premium here).
  - So policy-wide modifiers apply to the sum of item base premiums (not the sum after item-specific modifiers).
- Processing fee is added last.

### R7: Rounding
- Round in MHPCO's favor: premiums round UP, payouts round DOWN.
- Intermediate amounts are kept as fractions; only the final result is rounded.

### R8: Claim deductible
- 100 G deductible per damage event (per damaged item entry).

### R9: Claim cap
- Total payout per policy is capped at 2x the insurance sum.
- Insurance sum = sum of all items' insurance values (unmodified by premium modifiers).
- Component block discount affects premium only, not insurance sum.
- Cap persists across multiple claims on the same policy.

### R10: Claim special reimbursement clauses
- Enchantment >= 8: reimbursed at 50% of damage amount (before deductible).
- Dragon material: fully reimbursed.
- If both apply (dragon material AND enchantment >= 8): 50% rule wins.
- Components (runes, moonstones) have no enchantment level or material, so no special clause applies -- standard full reimbursement.

### R11: Claim payout calculation order
- Apply reimbursement clause (50% for high-enchantment, 100% for dragon material or standard).
- Subtract 100 G deductible.
- Clamp to remaining cap.
- Round down (MHPCO's favor).

### R12: Multiple items of the same type
- A policy can cover multiple items of the same type.
- Each damage entry is treated separately with its own deductible.
- If damages array has more entries of a given type than the policy covers, reject with non-zero exit code.

### R13: Sequential steps
- Steps are processed sequentially. A claim step references an earlier quote step by its zero-based index in the `policy` field.
- Multiple claims can reference the same policy; cap carries over.

### R14: CLI interface
- Entry point: `src/cli.ts`
- Reads JSON from stdin, writes JSON to stdout.
- Input: `{ customer: { yearsWithMHPCO: N }, steps: [...] }`
- Output: `{ results: [...] }` where each result is `{ premium: N }` for quote or `{ payout: N, remainingCap: N }` for claim.

### R15: Error handling
- Unknown item type in quote -> non-zero exit, error to stderr, no results to stdout.
- Damage entry referencing item not in policy -> non-zero exit, error to stderr.
- Unknown item type in damage -> non-zero exit, error to stderr.
- Negative damage amount -> non-zero exit, error to stderr.
- More damage entries of a type than the policy covers -> non-zero exit, error to stderr.

---

## Examples

### E1: Component base premiums
- 2 runes -> 50 G base premium (2 x 25)
- 3 runes -> 60 G base premium (block applies)
- 4 runes -> 100 G base premium (4 x 25, no block)
- 7 runes -> 175 G base premium (7 x 25, no block)

### E2: "Alike" means same type
- 2 runes + 1 moonstone -> 75 G (no block: different types)
- 3 runes + 3 moonstones -> 120 G (two blocks: 60 + 60)

### E3: Modifier scope on multi-item policies
- Cursed sword (base 100) + plain amulet (base 60) -> policy base 160 G; curse surcharge = 50 (50% of cursed sword's 100 G base) -> 210 G before further modifiers and fee.

### E4: Modifier thresholds
- Customer with exactly 2 years -> loyalty discount applies.
- Sword with enchantment 5 -> high-enchantment surcharge applies.
- Sword with enchantment 5 and cursed -> both surcharges apply.
- Sword with enchantment 4 -> no high-enchantment surcharge.
- Dragon-material sword, enchantment 8, damage 1000 G -> payout 400 G (50% = 500, minus 100 deductible).

### E5: Deductible per damage event
- Dragon attack damages sword (500 G) and amulet (300 G) -> payout 600 G (each gets its own 100 G deductible: (500-100) + (300-100) = 400 + 200 = 600).

### E6: Standard reimbursement
- Regular sword (steel, enchantment 3), damage 500 G -> payout 400 G (500 - 100).
- Rune, damage 200 G -> payout 100 G (200 - 100; no special clause).

### E7: Enchantment threshold vs. dragon material
- Dragon-material sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G -> payout 400 G (50% = 500, minus 100).
- Dragon-material sword, enchantment 5, damage 800 G -> payout 700 G (full reimbursement, minus 100).
- Steel sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G -> payout 400 G (50% = 500, minus 100).

### E8: Multiple items same type
- Two swords -> insurance sum 2000 G, cap 4000 G.
- Dragon attack damages both swords -> each damage entry treated separately with own deductible.
- More damage entries than insured items -> non-zero exit code, claim rejected.

### E9: Cap exhaustion
- Sword + amulet -> insurance sum 1600 G, cap 3200 G.
- Cursed sword (insurance 1000 G, premium 165 G) -> cap 2000 G (cap based on unmodified insurance value).
- Sword + 3 runes (block) -> insurance sum 1750 G (1000 + 3x250); block discount affects premium only.
- Sword insured (sum 1000 G, cap 2000 G); two successive claims of 1500 G each:
  - First claim -> payout 1400 G (1500 - 100), remaining cap 600 G.
  - Second claim -> payout 600 G (capped), remaining cap 0 G.

### E10: Rounding
- Premium yields 197.5 -> 198 G (round up).
- Payout yields 350.5 -> 350 G (round down).
- Intermediate fractions kept; only final value rounded.

### E11: Edge cases
- Empty item list -> premium 5 G (processing fee only).
- Unknown item type in quote -> non-zero exit, error to stderr.
- Damage entry for item not in policy -> non-zero exit, error to stderr.
- Negative damage amount -> non-zero exit, error to stderr.

### E12: Integration -- Newcomer with cursed sword
- Customer: 0 years, no previous contract.
- Item: cursed sword (steel, enchantment 3).
- Premium: 165 G = 100 base + 50 curse + 10 first insurance + 5 fee.

### E13: Integration -- Long-standing customer's second contract
- Customer: 3 years, second quote in scenario.
- Item: cursed sword (steel, enchantment 7).
- Premium: 160 G = 100 base + 50 curse + 30 high-enchantment - 20 loyalty + 10 first insurance - 15 follow-up + 5 fee.
- The first insurance surcharge applies to every item regardless of customer history.

---

## Questions / Clarifications

### Q1: "Alike" components (resolved)
"Alike" means exactly the same component type. Runes and moonstones are different types and do not form a block together.

### Q2: Modifier scope (resolved)
Item-specific modifiers (cursed, high enchantment) apply to the individual item's base premium. Policy-wide modifiers (loyalty, first insurance, follow-up) apply to the policy base premium (sum of all item base premiums, NOT the sum after item-specific modifiers).

### Q3: First insurance meaning (resolved)
"First insurance" surcharge applies per-item in every quote. Even long-standing customers on follow-up contracts pay it. It means each item in a quote is treated as a first insurance.

---

## Per-test Rationale

1. **empty item list -> premium 5 G** -- R5/E11: processing fee only
2. **single sword -> 100 G base premium** -- R1: base premium for sword
3. **single amulet -> 60 G base premium** -- R1: base premium for amulet
4. **single staff -> 80 G base premium** -- R1: base premium for staff
5. **single potion -> 40 G base premium** -- R1: base premium for potion
6. **single rune -> 25 G base premium** -- R1: component base premium
7. **2 runes -> 50 G base premium** -- E1: no block
8. **3 runes -> 60 G base premium** -- R2/E1: block applies
9. **4 runes -> 100 G base premium** -- R2/E1: no block (not exactly 3)
10. **7 runes -> 175 G base premium** -- R2/E1: no block
11. **2 runes + 1 moonstone -> 75 G** -- R2/E2/Q1: different types, no block
12. **3 runes + 3 moonstones -> 120 G** -- R2/E2: two separate blocks
13. **cursed sword -> +50% surcharge on item** -- R3: cursed modifier
14. **enchantment 5 sword -> +30% surcharge** -- R3/E4: high enchantment threshold
15. **enchantment 4 sword -> no surcharge** -- R3/E4: below threshold
16. **cursed + enchantment 5 -> both surcharges** -- R3/E4: both apply
17. **cursed sword + plain amulet -> 210 G before policy modifiers** -- R3/E3/Q2: cursed surcharge on item only
18. **loyalty discount for customer >= 2 years** -- R4/E4: loyalty threshold
19. **no loyalty discount for customer < 2 years** -- R4: below threshold
20. **first insurance surcharge +10%** -- R4: first insurance
21. **follow-up contract discount -15%** -- R4: second+ quote
22. **newcomer cursed sword integration -> 165 G** -- E12: full integration
23. **long-standing customer second contract -> 160 G** -- E13: full integration
24. **rounding premium up (197.5 -> 198)** -- R7/E10: round up
25. **rounding payout down (350.5 -> 350)** -- R7/E10: round down
26. **standard reimbursement: steel sword ench 3, damage 500 -> payout 400** -- R8/E6
27. **rune damage 200 -> payout 100** -- R8/E6: component, no special clause
28. **high enchantment claim (ench >= 8) -> 50% reimbursement** -- R10/E7
29. **dragon material -> full reimbursement** -- R10/E7
30. **dragon material + ench >= 8 -> 50% wins** -- R10/E7
31. **dragon material + ench 5 -> full reimbursement (800 -> 700)** -- R10/E7
32. **deductible per damage event** -- R8/E5: multiple damaged items
33. **two swords insured -> insurance sum 2000, cap 4000** -- R9/R12/E8
34. **cap = 2x insurance sum** -- R9/E9
35. **cap based on unmodified insurance value** -- R9/E9
36. **cap not affected by component block discount** -- R9/E9: sword + 3 runes = 1750 sum
37. **cap exhaustion across successive claims** -- R9/E9: first claim 1400, second capped at 600
38. **more damage entries than insured items -> error** -- R12/R15/E8
39. **unknown item type in quote -> error** -- R15/E11
40. **damage for item not in policy -> error** -- R15/E11
41. **negative damage amount -> error** -- R15/E11
42. **CLI reads JSON from stdin, writes JSON to stdout** -- R14: basic CLI
43. **CLI schema example: quote then claim** -- R14: full scenario
44. **CLI error exits with non-zero status code** -- R14/R15
45. **dragon-material sword ench 8 damage 1000 -> payout 400** -- E4: threshold example
46. **multiple items same type, each damage has own deductible** -- R12/E8

 Test Files  1 skipped (1)

**Test Cases** (ordered simple to complex):

Quote: base premiums (6 tests)
1. should return 5 G for an empty item list (processing fee only)
2. should return 105 G for a single sword (100 base + 5 fee)
3. should return 65 G for a single amulet (60 base + 5 fee)
4. should return 85 G for a single staff (80 base + 5 fee)
5. should return 45 G for a single potion (40 base + 5 fee)
6. should return 30 G for a single rune component (25 base + 5 fee)

Quote: component building block (4 tests)
7. should return 55 G for 2 runes (50 base + 5 fee, no block)
8. should return 65 G for 3 runes (60 base + 5 fee, block applies)
9. should return 105 G for 4 runes (100 base + 5 fee, no block)
10. should return 180 G for 7 runes (175 base + 5 fee, no block)

Quote: alike components means same type (2 tests)
11. should return 80 G for 2 runes + 1 moonstone (75 base + 5 fee, no block)
12. should return 125 G for 3 runes + 3 moonstones (120 base + 5 fee, two blocks)

Quote: item-specific modifiers (4 tests)
13. should add 50% cursed surcharge to the cursed item's base premium only
14. should add 30% high-enchantment surcharge for enchantment level >= 5
15. should not add high-enchantment surcharge for enchantment level 4
16. should apply both cursed and high-enchantment surcharges when both conditions met

Quote: modifier scope on multi-item policies (1 test)
17. cursed sword + plain amulet -- cursed surcharge applies to item only

Quote: policy-wide modifiers (5 tests)
18. should apply 20% loyalty discount when customer has >= 2 years
19. should apply loyalty discount when customer has exactly 2 years
20. should not apply loyalty discount when customer has < 2 years
21. should apply 10% first insurance surcharge on the policy base premium
22. should apply 15% follow-up contract discount on second quote

Quote: rounding (1 test)
23. should round premium UP (197.5 -> 198 G)

Quote: integration (2 tests)
24. newcomer with cursed sword -> 165 G
25. long-standing customer second contract -> 160 G

27. rune component, damage 200 -> payout 100

Claim: deductible per damage event (1 test)
32. sword (500) + amulet (300) -> payout 600

Claim: payout rounding (1 test)
33. round payout DOWN (350.5 -> 350)

Claim: cap (4 tests)
34. cap at 2x insurance sum
35. cap based on unmodified insurance value
36. component block discount does not affect insurance sum
37. cap at 4000 G for two swords

Claim: cap exhaustion (1 test)
38. successive claims exhaust cap: 1400 then 600

39. each damage entry treated separately with own deductible

42. damage entry for item not in policy
